title: Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares
essential_question: How can you measure the area of a shape by counting unit squares?

pretest.question: A rectangle is covered by 5 rows of unit squares. Each row has 3 squares. How many unit squares total?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Multiply rows by squares per row.

pretest.question: Why do we count area in ROWS and COLUMNS?
choices: A) It's faster than counting one by one
C) It always gives a wrong answer
D) It works only for triangles

B.note: You can also count one by one

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Multiplication beats counting one square at a time.

feedback.incorrect: Counting in rows × columns is faster than one-by-one.

pretest.question: A rectangle has 4 rows and 7 columns of unit squares. What is the area in square units?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: An L-shape is made of 7 unit squares (no gaps). What is the BEST way to find its area?
choices: A) Count each unit square one by one
C) Subtract from the largest rectangle
D) Both A and C work; B alone doesn't fit an L

A.note: Counting works, but C also works

B.note: Width × height alone is for rectangles

C.note: Subtracting works, but counting also works

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

feedback.incorrect: L-shapes work with counting or by subtracting from a bigger rectangle.

pretest.question: A rectangle has 8 columns and 2 rows of unit squares. What is the area in square units?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Counting Strategy
learn.content: To find the area of a shape covered with unit squares: COUNT them. We can count one by one for any shape, but for rectangles, we use a faster way: rows × columns.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Rows and Columns
learn.content: A ROW of unit squares goes ACROSS (left to right). A COLUMN goes UP and DOWN. A rectangle has rows AND columns of unit squares. The total = (number of rows) × (number of squares in one row).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Multiplication Shortcut for Area
learn.content: For rectangles: AREA = ROWS × COLUMNS (in unit squares). Example: 5 rows × 4 columns = 20 unit squares = 20 sq units. This is much faster than counting all 20 squares one at a time.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Counting Irregular Shapes
learn.content: For shapes that are NOT rectangles (like L-shapes, T-shapes), count the unit squares one by one. Or split the shape into smaller rectangles, find each area, then ADD.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Same Shape, Different Counts?
learn.content: If two students cover the SAME shape with NO gaps and NO overlaps, they both get the SAME area count. Area is fixed for a shape — it doesn't change based on who counts.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Smaller Squares = More Squares
learn.content: If you use SMALLER unit squares, you need MORE of them to cover the same shape. A rectangle that fits 12 unit squares of 1×1 might fit 48 squares of 1/2 × 1/2 — but the area is still the same in standard units.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Use a Strategy
learn.content: Choose the best counting strategy: (1) RECTANGLE → rows × columns. (2) IRREGULAR → count one by one OR split into rectangles. (3) Always check no gaps and no overlaps.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today: (1) Count unit squares to find area. (2) For rectangles: AREA = rows × columns. (3) For irregular: count or split into rectangles. (4) Check no gaps, no overlaps. (5) Area is the same no matter who counts (with same units).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.2 'Measure Area by Counting Unit Squares'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

practice_r3.question: A rectangle has area 32 sq units. The number of rows is DOUBLE the number of columns. Find rows + columns.
solution_steps: Step 2: rows × cols = 32. So 2 × cols × cols = 32.

16.note: 16 is cols squared, not rows + cols

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: What's the relationship between rows and cols? Read 2: What's the area? Read 3: Find cols, then rows, then sum.

hints: If rows = 2 × cols and rows × cols = 32, find cols × cols.
